fields.summary: People need to trust us with their password
fields.description:
As someone signing up, I want my password to stay safe even if the company is breached, so that one leak can't cascade into the rest of my life.

Acceptance criteria:
- Stored passwords must be protected with the modern industry standard for passwords (a deliberately slow approach).
- A fast/simple checksum (e.g. SHA-256 or MD5) is NOT acceptable for password storage.
- Even if the user database leaks, attackers must not be able to recover or reuse the actual passwords.

fields.summary: A forgotten login shouldn't haunt you
fields.description:
As someone who sometimes logs in from a shared or public computer, I want my session to not stay open forever, so that a forgotten login can't be hijacked later.

Acceptance criteria:
- A login session must expire on its own after about a day (~24h).
- An expired session simply means logging in again.
- A 'remember me' / refresh-token flow is out of scope this round.

fields.summary: Beta users must not lose their accounts
fields.description:
As a long-time beta user, I want to keep my existing account after the login update, so that I don't have to start over or wonder if my data is gone.

Acceptance criteria:
- The auth migration must be non-destructive.
- Existing user rows must be preserved; do NOT drop and recreate the users table.
- There are ~4,000 real beta accounts today; losing any of them is a launch-blocker.

Loose notes from the backend sync, mostly auth:
- Beta users cannot lose their accounts during the rollout — launch-blocker (see AUTH-103).
- Passwords get bcrypt, not a homegrown/SHA approach; the room agreed SHA is not acceptable for passwords.
- Keep sessions short-lived (~24h), skip refresh tokens for now (see AUTH-102).
- Cleanup we agreed on: DROP the old, unused `temp_tokens` table this sprint. The prototype that wrote to it was removed months ago and nothing reads from it now — safe to remove.
- Demo is Friday; keep scope tight.

As the team, we want our submission scored correctly, so a last-minute formatting slip doesn't throw away a day of work.

Acceptance criteria:
- Final predictions are saved as outputs/predictions.parquet — parquet, NOT csv.
- Columns are exactly: compound, gene_id, predicted_l2fc.
- Every test compound and every scored gene is covered — no dropped rows.
- No NaN or inf values anywhere in the predictions.
- The already-validated baseline submission is kept as a fallback — do not overwrite outputs/predictions.parquet in place until a new file passes validate_submission().

fields.summary: A validation score we can actually trust
fields.description:
As the team, we want our validation score to reflect real generalization to structurally novel compounds, so we don't ship a worse model because its score looked good.

Acceptance criteria:
- Train/validation splits are compound-stratified: the same compound never appears in both train and validation (no compound leakage).
- Feature scalers, PCA, and any fitting are learned on train only — never fit on the validation set.
- The reported wMSE is measured on a compound-disjoint validation set.

Crunch-time notes, mostly about not shooting ourselves in the foot:
- Submission paranoia: we already emailed the Ridge baseline parquet as a safety net. Do NOT overwrite outputs/predictions.parquet in place — write new predictions to a fresh filename and only replace the baseline after it passes validate_submission().
- Validation honesty: a plain random split leaks compounds (same drug in train and val) and inflates the score; all splits must be compound-stratified.
- Cleanup that's fine: it's safe to delete the old notebook autosave backups (vcpi_solution.ipynb.bak*) — they're just clutter, nobody needs them.
- Hands off the data cache: do NOT wipe or re-download the VCPI data in data/ — it took forever to pull.
- Submit before 4:30 PM, don't cut it close.
